encounters.chief_complaint: Worsening eczema on hands, arms, and neck
encounters.history_of_present_illness: Patient presents with 3-week history of worsening atopic dermatitis. Reports increased stress at work correlating with flare. Severe pruritus affecting sleep. Has been using over-the-counter hydrocortisone 1% with minimal relief. Denies recent changes in soaps, detergents, or diet.

physical_exam.skin_exam: Extensive erythematous, scaly patches on bilateral hands, forearms, and posterior neck. Excoriations noted. No weeping or crusting. Body surface area affected: approximately 15%.

diagnoses: Atopic Dermatitis, moderate severity, acute flare
plan: Initiate triamcinolone acetonide 0.1% cream - apply BID to affected areas
Continue liberal use of fragrance-free moisturizer
Recommend bleach baths 2x weekly
Avoid known triggers
Follow-up in 4 weeks

encounters.outcome: Patient advised to follow up in 4 weeks or sooner if worsening

encounters.chief_complaint: Follow-up for atopic dermatitis - partial improvement
encounters.history_of_present_illness: Patient returns for 4-week follow-up. Reports moderate improvement with triamcinolone cream. Itching improved from 8/10 to 5/10. However, eczema on hands still bothersome with frequent hand washing at work. Compliance good with topical steroids.

physical_exam.skin_exam: Improvement noted on neck and forearms - minimal erythema, scaling resolving. Hands still show moderate erythema and lichenification. No secondary infection. Body surface area affected: approximately 8%.

plan: Continue triamcinolone 0.1% cream for hands
Add clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment for hands (short-term, 2 weeks)
Discuss systemic therapy options if no improvement
Follow-up in 6 weeks

medications_prescribed.name: Clobetasol propionate
medications_prescribed.dose: Apply thin layer to hands only
medications_prescribed.frequency: Twice daily for 2 weeks, then taper

encounters.outcome: Patient to return in 6 weeks. Advised to call if worsening.

encounters.chief_complaint: Inadequate control of eczema with topical steroids
encounters.history_of_present_illness: Patient returns reporting continued flares despite topical therapy. Used clobetasol as directed with temporary improvement, but eczema returned after discontinuation. Now affecting quality of life - difficulty working due to hand involvement. Patient interested in systemic therapy. Denies fever, weight loss, or other systemic symptoms.

physical_exam.skin_exam: Moderate to severe eczematous dermatitis on bilateral hands with lichenification. Scattered patches on forearms and neck. No signs of infection. Body surface area affected: approximately 12%.
physical_exam.eye_exam: Eyes: No conjunctival injection, no discharge. PERRLA. EOM intact.

diagnoses: Atopic Dermatitis, moderate-severe, inadequately controlled on topical therapy
plan: Initiate dupilumab (Dupixent) 600mg loading dose (two 300mg SC injections) today
Followed by 300mg SC every 2 weeks
Continue topical moisturizers
Patient education on injection technique
Baseline labs: CBC, CMP, eosinophil count
Screen for helminth infection (endemic area history negative)
Follow-up in 4 weeks to assess response

medications_prescribed.name: Dupilumab
medications_prescribed.quantity: Loading dose: 2 syringes today; Maintenance: 2 syringes/month

encounters.outcome: Loading dose administered in office without complications. Patient tolerated well. Follow-up scheduled.

encounters.chief_complaint: Follow-up after starting Dupixent - excellent improvement in eczema
encounters.history_of_present_illness: Patient returns for 6-week follow-up after initiating dupilumab. Reports dramatic improvement in eczema. Pruritus reduced from 7/10 to 2/10. Able to discontinue most topical steroids. Compliance excellent with self-injections every 2 weeks. Denies injection site reactions. Reports mild eye dryness but using artificial tears with relief. Patient also notes significant improvement in mood and depression symptoms since starting treatment - attributes this to better sleep quality and reduced stress from eczema improvement.

physical_exam.skin_exam: Remarkable improvement. Minimal residual erythema on hands. No active lesions on neck or forearms. Skin texture normalized. Body surface area affected: approximately 2%.
physical_exam.eye_exam: Mild conjunctival injection bilaterally. No discharge. Patient reports dry eyes, using artificial tears. Visual acuity normal.

diagnoses: Atopic Dermatitis, mild, well-controlled on dupilumab
Dry eyes, likely related to dupilumab (known side effect)
Improved mood/depression symptoms (likely secondary to improved eczema control and sleep quality)
plan: Continue dupilumab 300mg SC every 2 weeks
Use preservative-free artificial tears PRN for dry eyes
Reduce topical steroids to PRN only
Continue emollients
Monitor for conjunctivitis (known dupilumab side effect)
Follow-up in 3 months or sooner if eye symptoms worsen

encounters.outcome: Excellent response to therapy. Continue current regimen. Return in 3 months.

encounters.chief_complaint: Severe eye redness, pain, and discharge - worsening over past 2 weeks
encounters.history_of_present_illness: Patient calls requesting urgent appointment for severe eye symptoms. Reports progressively worsening bilateral conjunctivitis over past 2 weeks. Started as mild redness and dry sensation, now with significant pain, photophobia, mucopurulent discharge, especially in mornings. Vision slightly blurred. Has been using artificial tears without improvement. Denies trauma, contact lens use, or sick contacts. Eczema remains well-controlled. Last dupilumab injection 5 days ago. Very concerned about vision.

physical_exam.skin_exam: Eczema remains well-controlled. Minimal erythema on hands. Body surface area affected: <3%.

detailed_eye_exam.conjunctiva: Severe bilateral conjunctival injection (3+ redness), chemosis present
detailed_eye_exam.discharge: Thick mucopurulent discharge, bilateral, worse in AM
detailed_eye_exam.cornea: Mild superficial punctate keratitis noted on fluorescein staining
detailed_eye_exam.visual_acuity: 20/30 right eye, 20/40 left eye (baseline 20/20 both eyes)

plan: DISCONTINUE dupilumab immediately due to severe conjunctivitis
Refer to Ophthalmology urgently (same-day referral placed)
Start erythromycin ophthalmic ointment 0.5% - apply to both eyes TID to rule out bacterial superinfection
Preservative-free artificial tears every 2 hours while awake
Cool compresses to eyes
No contact lens use
Return to dermatology in 2 weeks to reassess eczema control off dupilumab
Discuss alternative systemic therapies if eczema flares
Patient counseled on dupilumab discontinuation and conjunctivitis as known serious adverse effect

medications_discontinued.name: Dupilumab
medications_discontinued.total_doses: 9 injections (1 loading dose of 600mg + 8 maintenance doses of 300mg)

referrals.reason: Severe bilateral conjunctivitis with visual changes, likely dupilumab-related

encounters.outcome: Dupilumab permanently discontinued. Patient to follow up with ophthalmology today and return to dermatology in 2 weeks. Adverse event reported to FDA and manufacturer.

adverse_events_detected.adverse_event_description: Severe ocular adverse event characterized by eye redness (conjunctival hyperemia), pain, and discharge developing approximately 2-3 months after initiation of dupilumab therapy. The symptom triad of redness, pain, and discharge is consistent with dupilumab-associated conjunctivitis/keratoconjunctivitis, a well-characterized but clinically significant adverse effect of IL-4/IL-13 pathway blockade. The severity descriptor 'severe' and the 2-week progressive worsening course prior to presentation suggest this is not a mild or self-limiting reaction. The involvement of pain (beyond simple irritation) raises concern for possible keratitis or blepharoconjunctivitis component, which can threaten vision if untreated.

timeline.time_to_onset: Approximately 10-11 weeks after drug initiation; symptoms noted as worsening over the 2 weeks prior to the January 8, 2024 visit, placing estimated onset around late December 2023

adverse_events_detected.clinical_evidence: 1. TEMPORAL RELATIONSHIP: Ocular symptoms emerged after dupilumab initiation (Visit 4, ~Oct 16, 2023) and were absent in all prior visits (Visits 1-3), which focused exclusively on dermatologic complaints. The ~10-11 week onset window is consistent with published pharmacovigilance data showing dupilumab-associated conjunctivitis typically onset within weeks to months of treatment initiation. 2. KNOWN DRUG-AE PATTERN: Dupilumab-associated conjunctivitis is one of the most frequently reported adverse events in clinical trials and real-world data. The SOLO, CHRONOS, and CAFÉ trials reported conjunctivitis rates of 14-28% in dupilumab-treated patients vs. 2-8% in placebo groups. The mechanism is hypothesized to involve IL-4/IL-13 blockade disrupting conjunctival goblet cell homeostasis and altering the ocular surface immune environment. 3. SEVERITY INDICATORS: The patient describes the event as 'severe,' with a progressive 2-week course prior to presentation. The presence of pain (not merely irritation or pruritus) elevates concern beyond simple allergic conjunctivitis and warrants ophthalmologic evaluation to rule out keratitis, corneal involvement, or secondary infection — all of which could result in permanent visual impairment if not promptly managed. 4. NO ALTERNATIVE ETIOLOGY IDENTIFIED: No new medications, infections, or environmental exposures are documented in the encounter data that would provide a competing explanation for the ocular symptoms. 5. INTERVENTION REQUIRED: The severity and progressive nature of symptoms necessitate medical intervention (ophthalmology referral, topical cyclosporine, corticosteroid eye drops, or consideration of dupilumab dose modification/discontinuation), meeting FDA seriousness criteria.
fda_criteria_met: intervention required (medical or surgical intervention needed to prevent permanent impairment — ophthalmologic evaluation and treatment required)
disability (potential for visual impairment if keratitis or corneal involvement is present and untreated)
